description: Flowers are replaced by seed capsules about 1/3 of an inch long that are lanceoloid in shape and 5-lobed. The interior of each seed capsule has 5 completely separated cells; there are numerous seeds in each cell. These seeds are dark-colored, narrowly oblongoid, and somewhat flattened. The capsule turns dark red as it matures.

description: Hypericum perforatum, commonly known as St. John's wort, is a flowering plant in the family Hypericaceae. It is a hairless, perennial herb with woody roots, yellow flowers marked by black glands, and leaves that appear perforated due to translucent glands, producing thousands of seeds per plant.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.toxicity_note: Toxic to Horses. Signs: Photosensitization (sunburn, dermatitis)
care.toxicity_first_aid: Remove any remaining plant material from mouth. Rinse with water. Monitor for symptoms (vomiting, drooling, lethargy). Call poison control or vet if symptoms appear.
care.used_for: ["Native Americans have been known to use this plant for medicinal purposes.  Flowers can be used as a yellow dye plant."]
care.used_for_details: Native Americans have been known to use this plant for medicinal purposes.  Flowers can be used as a yellow dye plant.
care.edible_parts: Ingestion of St John's wort can reduce the effectiveness of prescription medicine.
